Energy resource center

Aviso: Los recursos en español se encuentran más abajo en esta página.

Electricity powers virtually everything we do. That's why it's important to know how electricity is generated and distributed so we can understand the vital importance of this critical infrastructure.  

We're taking steps to expand our renewable energy capacity and build a safe, resilient energy system for the future. You can help lower your energy use by participating in a variety of programs.

Your choices for savings and more

Incentives and rebates

Get Wattsmart® rebates when you make energy-efficient upgrades to your home or business.

We offer bill assistance and payment plans to customers experiencing financial hardships.

Support renewable energy for as little as $1.95 a month when you sign up for the Blue Sky® program.

New programs in Washington support transportation electrification. Find out more about our Communities grants and technical assistance.

Renewable energy

Electric mobility

Helping businesses thrive

Electricity 101

How is electricity generated?

The electricity we use every day is generated in power plants by converting natural resources such as water, sun, coal, oil, natural gas and wind into electrical power.

There are two general categories of natural resources—non-renewable and renewable. A non-renewable resource is not capable of being renewed, replaced or takes a very long time to replace. A renewable resource is capable of being renewed or replaced.

  • Non-renewable resource examples are coal, oil, natural gas and uranium. 
  • Renewable resource examples are solar, wind, geothermal and hydropower. 

Electricity is generated by a large magnet that spins inside coils of wire. High-pressure steam or water is used to turn a turbine—a large fan—attached to the generator to get the magnet spinning. As the magnet rotates inside the loops of wire, electric current is produced.

How is electricity delivered?

Electricity is sent through a vast power grid of high voltage transmission lines to a substation in your area. At the substation, the voltage is lowered, then overhead or underground distribution lines carry the electricity to your home or work.

Power lines leading to residential areas generally carry 4,200 to 34,500 volts. Within neighborhoods, the voltage is decreased further with step-down transformers located on the top of poles (or in a vault on the ground) to the typical 120 to 240 volts for safe home use.

How does electricity work?

Although electricity is invisible, the earth’s atmosphere is charged with it. You’ll see proof of its presence during a lightning storm.

Electricity's physical properties

  • Electricity always seeks a path to the ground. 
  • Electricity travels at the speed of light.
  • Electricity is attracted to materials known as “conductors” that allow it to flow readily. Some conductors include:
    • Metal
    • Water
    • Wet objects
    • Trees (they contain moisture)
    • People (bodies contain water)
    • Dirt on insulators
  • Unlike conductors, electricity does not flow easily through certain insulating materials, such as:
    • Rubber
    • Glass
    • Plastic
    • Porcelain

How can I stay safe around electricity?

Electrical-related accidents can occur anywhere and anytime a person or conductor interrupts the flow of the current, providing it a path to the ground. Most of the time, using common sense and extra caution around electricity will prevent such accidents.

Ways to stay safe around electricity:

  • Look up for overhead lines before starting an outdoor project. Always keep yourself and anything you are handling at least 10 feet away from overhead power lines.
  • Call 811 before you dig to have underground lines marked. 
  • Stay away from power lines and treat any line you see as being energized and dangerous. 
  • Inside your home, be sure to "childproof" electrical outlets, discard appliances or lights with worn or frayed cords, avoid circuit overloading and use ground fault circuit interrupter devices (GFCIs). GFCIs are outlets equipped with "test" and "reset" buttons near water sources.

Energy options

Time of Use

Blue Sky renewable energy

Washington Clean Energy Implementation Plan

The Clean Energy Implementation Plan is a near-term four-year action plan specific to Washington customers and communities that focuses on community-based actions to move toward meeting Washington's Clean Energy Transformation Act's milestones. 

Learn more through our infographic.

Opciones de energía

Tiempo de Uso

Energía renovable

Plan de Implementación de Energías Limpias

El Clean Energy Implementation Plan es un plan de acción a corto plazo, de cuatro años, específico para los clientes y comunidades de Washington que se enfoca en acciones basadas en la comunidad para avanzar en el logro de los hitos establecidos en la CETA.
